name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَضَلَّ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (١)
```

```english
Those who are [themselves] faithless and bar [others] from the way of Allah—He has rendered their works fruitless. (1)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَءَامَنُوا۟ بِمَا نُزِّلَ عَلَىٰ مُحَمَّدٍ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۙ كَفَّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ وَأَصْلَحَ بَالَهُمْ (٢)
```

```english
But those who have faith and do righteous deeds and believe in what has been sent down to Muhammad—and it is the truth from their Lord—He shall absolve them of their misdeeds and set right their affairs. (2)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ وَأَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ أَمْثَـٰلَهُمْ (٣)
```

```english
That is because the faithless follow falsehood, and the faithful follow the truth from their Lord. That is how Allah draws comparisons for mankind. (3)
```

```arabic
فَإِذَا لَقِيتُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَضَرْبَ ٱلرِّقَابِ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَثْخَنتُمُوهُمْ فَشُدُّوا۟ ٱلْوَثَاقَ فَإِمَّا مَنًّۢا بَعْدُ وَإِمَّا فِدَآءً حَتَّىٰ تَضَعَ ٱلْحَرْبُ أَوْزَارَهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ وَلَوْ يَشَآءُ ٱللَّهُ لَٱنتَصَرَ مِنْهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَبْلُوَا۟ بَعْضَكُم بِبَعْضٍ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَلَن يُضِلَّ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٤)
```

```english
When you meet the faithless in battle, strike their necks. When you have thoroughly decimated them, bind the captives firmly. Thereafter either oblige them [by setting them free] or take ransom, until the war lays down its burdens. That [is Allah’s ordinance]. Had Allah wished He could have taken vengeance on them, but that He may test some of you by means of others. As for those who were slain in the way of Allah, He will not let their works go fruitless. (4)
```

```arabic
سَيَهْدِيهِمْ وَيُصْلِحُ بَالَهُمْ (٥)
```

```english
He will guide them and set right their affairs, (5)
```

```arabic
وَيُدْخِلُهُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةَ عَرَّفَهَا لَهُمْ (٦)
```

```english
and admit them into paradise, with which He has acquainted them. (6)
```

 thoughtful Standard cleaning and formatting applied. No commentary added. Consistent terminology used.

## (Commentary)

The Surah describes the disbelievers (الذين كفروا) with the vile attributes and evil deeds that characterize them, and describes the believers (الذين آمنوا) with their goodly traits and righteous deeds. It then mentions the consequences that follow the attributes of the latter—in terms of blessing and honor—and the attributes of the former—in terms of vengeance and humiliation. In general, the Surah presents a comparison between the two groups regarding their attributes and actions in this world and what results from them in the Hereafter. It also contains some rulings related to fighting (القتال).

It is a Medinan Surah, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَضَلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ** — **"As for those who are faithless and bar [others] from the way of Allah, He has let their deeds go astray."** (47:1). Turning away (الصد) has been interpreted as averting oneself from the way of Allah, which is Islam, as reported from some commentators. Others have interpreted it as prevention (المنع), meaning their preventing people from believing in the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — to which the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم was calling them.

The second interpretation is more in accord with the context of the subsequent verses, especially those commanding the believers to kill and capture them.

Thus, what is meant by "those who are faithless" (الذين كفروا) are the disbelievers of Mecca and those who followed them in their disbelief. They used to prevent people from believing in the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم and would persecute them; they also barred them from the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام).

His saying: **أَضَلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ** — **"He has let their deeds go astray"** means He made their deeds wander, such that they do not reach the objectives they intended, which were, in short, to nullify the truth and revive falsehood. Thus, the sentence carries the same meaning as His frequently repeated saying: **وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكافِرِينَ** — **"and Allah does not guide the faithless lot."** (2:264). Indeed, the Glorified has promised to establish the truth and nullify falsehood, as in His saying: **لِيُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ وَيُبْطِلَ الْباطِلَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُجْرِمُونَ** — **"that He might confirm the truth and bring falsehood to naught, though the guilty should be averse."** (8:8).

Therefore, the meaning of the "straying" (ضلال) of their deeds is their futility and corruption without reaching their goal. Describing this as "straying" is a form of metaphorical allusion (استعارة بالكناية).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَآمَنُوا بِما نُزِّلَ عَلى مُحَمَّدٍ وَهُوَ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ** — **"But those who have faith and do righteous deeds and believe in what has been sent down to Muhammad—and it is the truth from their Lord—"** (47:2) etc. The apparent generality of the beginning of the verse suggests that "those who have faith" refers to anyone who believes and performs righteous deeds (الصالحات). Thus, His saying: **وَآمَنُوا بِما نُزِّلَ عَلى مُحَمَّدٍ** — **"and believe in what has been sent down to Muhammad"** would be a restrictive qualification (تقييد احترازي) rather than mere emphasis, highlighting that which is the primary object of concern in faith.

His saying: **وَهُوَ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ** — **"and it is the truth from their Lord"** is a parenthetical clause (جملة معترضة), and the pronoun refers back to "what has been sent down" (ما نزل).

His saying: **كَفَّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ وَأَصْلَحَ بالَهُمْ** — **"He will absolve them of their misdeeds and set right their state."** (47:2). It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "The state (البال) refers to the condition (الحال) and affair (الشأن). *Al-Bal* also means the heart (القلب); it is said: 'Such a thing occurred to my *bal* (mind).' The word *bal* is not used in the plural because it is more ambiguous than its counterparts like *hal* and *shan*." End quote.

The "letting deeds go astray" in the previous verse has been contrasted with the "absolving of misdeeds" and "setting right the state" in this verse. This means guiding their faith and righteous deeds to the ultimate goal of felicity (السعادة). This is only completed by the absolution of misdeeds that prevent one from reaching felicity; for this reason, the absolution of misdeeds was joined with the setting right of their state.

The meaning is: Allah has drawn a veil over their misdeeds through pardon and forgiveness, and has set right their state in this world and the Hereafter. As for this world, it is because the true religion is that which accords with the requirements of the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being*. The primordial nature only requires that which contains its felicity and perfection. Thus, in believing in what Allah has sent down of the religion of nature and acting upon it, there is the rectification of the believers' state in their worldly society. As for the Hereafter, it is because it is the consequence of the worldly life; since its beginning was felicitous, its end shall be likewise. The Exalted said: **وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِلتَّقْوى** — **"and the outcome will be in favor of Godwariness."** (20:132).

His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا اتَّبَعُوا الْباطِلَ وَأَنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّبَعُوا الْحَقَّ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ** — **"That is because the faithless follow falsehood, and the faithful follow the truth from their Lord."** (47:3) etc. This is an explanation of the cause (تعليل) for what was mentioned in the two preceding verses regarding the straying of the disbelievers' deeds and the rectification of the believers' state along with the absolution of their misdeeds.

In qualifying the truth with the phrase **مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ** — **"from their Lord"**, there is an indication that what is attributed to the Exalted is the truth, while falsehood has no attribution to Him. Therefore, the Glorified took charge of setting right the state of the believers because the path of truth they followed is attributed to Him. As for the disbelievers and their deeds, the Exalted has no concern for them. As for the attribution of their straying to Him in His saying: "He has let their deeds go astray," the meaning of letting their deeds go astray is His not guiding them to righteous and felicitous ends.

The verse contains an indication that the sole criterion for human felicity or wretchedness is the following of truth or the following of falsehood. The reason for this is the attribution of truth to the Exalted, to the exclusion of falsehood.

His saying: **كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللهُ لِلنَّاسِ أَمْثالَهُمْ** — **"Thus does Allah draw for mankind their parables."** (47:3) means He clarifies their descriptions to them as they truly are. The use of the demonstrative pronoun for the distant (ذلك) serves to magnify the matter of the parable He has struck.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَضَرْبَ الرِّقابِ** — **"So when you meet the faithless [in battle], strike their necks"** (47:4) until the end of the verse. This is a derivation (تفريع) from what preceded in the three verses regarding the description of the two groups. It is as if it were said: Since the believers are the people of truth and Allah bestows upon them what He bestows, and the disbelievers are the people of falsehood and Allah lets their deeds go astray, then the believers, when they meet the disbelievers, must kill and capture them so that the truth upon which the believers stand may live, and the earth may be purified from the falsehood upon which the disbelievers stand.

His saying: **فَإِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَضَرْبَ الرِّقابِ** — **"So when you meet the faithless [in battle], strike their necks"**: "Meeting" (اللقاء) here refers to meeting in combat. "Striking the necks" (ضرب الرقاب) is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) standing in place of its governing verb. The implied meaning is: "Strike the necks—meaning their necks—with a striking." Striking the neck is a metaphor for killing with the sword, because the easiest and swiftest way to kill is by striking the neck with it.

His saying: **حَتَّى إِذا أَثْخَنْتُمُوهُمْ فَشُدُّوا الْوَثاقَ** — **"until when you have subdued them, then bind them fast."** (47:4). It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "Subduing (الإثخان) is to kill extensively and to overcome and conquer the enemy. From it is said: 'Illness subdued him' when it became severe, and 'wounds subdued him'." End quote. In *al-Mufradat*: "I trusted (وثقت) in him... I relied upon him. I bound him (أوثقته) means I tied him firmly. The word *al-wathaq* (الوثاق)—with a *fatha* or *kasra* on the *waw*—are two nouns for that with which a thing is bound." End quote. "Until" (حتى) marks the limit (غاية) for striking the necks. The meaning is: Kill them until you have killed extensively among them, then take them as captives by binding them fast and securely. Thus, "binding them fast" refers to taking captives (الأسر). The verse, in making the taking of captives contingent upon subduing the enemy, carries the same meaning as His saying: **ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى حَتَّى يُثْخِنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ** — **"A prophet may not take captives until he has thoroughly subdued [the enemy] in the land."** (8:67).

His saying: **فَإِمَّا مَنًّا بَعْدُ وَإِمَّا فِداءً** — **"then either [set them free] with a favor afterwards or [let them go] for a ransom"** (47:4) means: capture them, and it follows from this that you either show them favor after the capture by releasing them or enslaving them, or you ransom them for wealth or in exchange for the captives they hold of yours.

His saying: **حَتَّى تَضَعَ الْحَرْبُ أَوْزارَها** — **"until the war lays down its burdens."** (47:4). The "burdens" (أوزار) of war are its heavy loads, namely the weapons carried by the combatants. What is meant is the combatants and people of war laying down their weapons, which is a metaphor for the end of the fighting.

From the preceding meaning, the error in the claim of some that this verse abrogates (ناسخة) His saying: **ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى حَتَّى يُثْخِنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ** — **"A prophet may not take captives until he has thoroughly subdued [the enemy] in the land"** (8:67) becomes clear. Their argument was that this Surah was revealed later than Surah al-Anfal and thus abrogates it.

This is incorrect because there is no contradiction between the two verses. The verse in al-Anfal prohibits taking captives before subduing the enemy, while the verse under discussion commands taking captives after subduing them.

Similarly, the claim that His saying: **فَشُدُّوا الْوَثاقَ** — **"then bind them fast"** etc., is abrogated by the "Verse of the Sword": **فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ** — **"then slay the polytheists wherever you find them"** (9:5). This seems to be based on the view that a general rule (العام) revealed after a specific one (الخاص) abrogates it rather than being specified by it. The truth is contrary to this, and the full discussion belongs to the Principles of Jurisprudence (أصول الفقه). The verse also contains jurisprudential discussions whose place is the science of Jurisprudence (الفقه).

His saying: **ذلِكَ** — **"That [is the ordinance]"** (47:4) means the matter is thus; that is, the judgment of Allah is what has been mentioned in the verse.

His saying: **وَلَوْ يَشاءُ اللهُ لَانْتَصَرَ مِنْهُمْ** — **"and had Allah wished He would have taken vengeance on them"** (47:4). The pronoun refers to the disbelievers. That is, had Allah willed to take vengeance upon them, He would have done so by destroying them and punishing them without commanding you to fight them.

His saying: **وَلكِنْ لِيَبْلُوَا بَعْضَكُمْ بِبَعْضٍ** — **"but [He desires] to test some of you by means of others."** (47:4) is an emendation (استدراك) regarding the will to take vengeance. That is, He did not take vengeance upon them directly, but rather commanded you to fight them to test some of you by means of others. Thus, He tests the believers by means of the disbelievers—commanding them to fight them so that the obedient may be distinguished from the disobedient—and He tests the disbelievers by means of the believers, so that the people of wretchedness among them may be distinguished from those who are granted the success to repent from falsehood and return to the truth.

It has thus become clear that His saying: **لِيَبْلُوَا بَعْضَكُمْ بِبَعْضٍ** — **"to test some of you by means of others"** is the explanation of the cause for the rulings mentioned in the verse. The address in "some of you" (بعضكم) is to the collective of believers and disbelievers, though the speech is directed toward the believers.

His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ فَلَنْ يُضِلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ** — **"As for those who are slain in the way of Allah, He will never let their deeds go astray."** (47:4). The speech is phrased as a conditional, and the ruling is general. That is, whoever is killed in the way of Allah—which is struggle (الجهاد) and fighting against the enemies of the religion—He will never nullify the righteous deeds they performed in the way of Allah.

It has been said that what is meant by "those who are slain in the way of Allah" are the martyrs of the Day of Uhud. However, this is a specification without a specifier, while the context is one of generality.

His saying, the Exalted: **سَيَهْدِيهِمْ وَيُصْلِحُ بالَهُمْ** — **"He will guide them and set right their state,"** (47:5). The pronoun refers to those who were killed in the way of Allah. Thus, the verse and the one following it are to clarify their condition after martyrdom. That is, Allah will guide them to the stations of felicity and honor, and set right their state through forgiveness and pardoning their misdeeds, so they become fit to enter Paradise.

When this verse is joined with His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ أَمْواتاً بَلْ أَحْياءٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ** — **"Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead; no, they are living with their Lord,"** (3:169), it becomes clear that the meaning of "setting right their state" is reviving them with a life through which they are fit to be present with their Lord by the lifting of the veil.

It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "The reason for the repetition of His saying: 'their state' (بالهم) is that the first instance meant He set right their state in religion and the world, while the second means He will set right their state in the bliss of the Hereafter. Thus, the first is the cause of bliss, and the second is the bliss itself." End quote. The difference between the meaning he mentioned and what we have presented is that His saying: **وَيُصْلِحُ بالَهُمْ** — **"and set right their state"**, according to what we mentioned, is like an explanatory apposition (عطف تفسيري) to His saying: **سَيَهْدِيهِمْ** — **"He will guide them"**, unlike what he mentioned. Furthermore, the subsequent saying: **وَيُدْخِلُهُمُ الْجَنَّةَ** — **"and admit them into paradise"** is, according to what he mentioned, like an explanatory apposition to His saying: **وَيُصْلِحُ بالَهُمْ**, unlike what we mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَيُدْخِلُهُمُ الْجَنَّةَ عَرَّفَها لَهُمْ** — **"and admit them into paradise, which He has made known to them."** (47:6). This is the ultimate goal of His guiding them. His saying: **عَرَّفَها لَهُمْ** — **"which He has made known to them"** is a circumstantial clause (حال) regarding His admitting them into Paradise. That is, He will admit them into Paradise while He has already made it known to them, either through worldly clarification via revelation and prophethood, or through glad tidings at the time of death, or in the grave, or on the Day of Resurrection, or in all of these stages. This is the meaning suggested by the context.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from ‘Ali who said: "A verse in Surah Muhammad is about us, and a verse is about the Umayyads."

I say: Al-Qummi narrated in his *Tafsir* from his father, from some of our companions, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), a similar report.

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying: **فَإِذا لَقِيتُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَضَرْبَ الرِّقابِ** — **"So when you meet the faithless [in battle], strike their necks"** etc., it is narrated from the Imams of Guidance (peace be upon them): "Captives are of two types: a type taken before the end of the fighting while the war is still ongoing—in this case, the Imam has the choice between killing them or cutting off their hands and feet on opposite sides and leaving them until they bleed out; showing favor (المن) or ransom (الفداء) is not permissible.

The other type are those taken after the war has laid down its burdens and the fighting has ended—in this case, the Imam has the choice regarding them between showing favor, ransom—either with wealth or exchange of persons—enslavement, or striking the necks. If they embrace Islam in either case, all of that is dropped, and their ruling becomes that of the Muslims."

I say: A report with this meaning is narrated in *al-Kafi* from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Jurayj regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ فَلَنْ يُضِلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ** — **"As for those who are slain in the way of Allah, He will never let their deeds go astray"** (47:4). He said: "It was revealed concerning those of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم who were killed on the Day of Uhud."

I say: You have already known that the verse is general. The use of the future tense (سياق الاستقبال) in His saying: **سَيَهْدِيهِمْ وَيُصْلِحُ بالَهُمْ** — **"He will guide them and set right their state"** etc., only accords with generality and the fact that the speech is intended to establish a general principle.

It has been narrated that His saying, the Exalted: **حَتَّى إِذا أَثْخَنْتُمُوهُمْ فَشُدُّوا الْوَثاقَ** — **"until when you have subdued them, then bind them fast"** (47:4) abrogates His saying: **ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى** — **"A prophet may not take captives..."** (8:67). Also, that His saying: **فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ** — **"then slay the polytheists wherever you find them"** (9:5) abrogates His saying: **فَشُدُّوا الْوَثاقَ فَإِمَّا مَنًّا بَعْدُ وَإِمَّا فِداءً** — **"then bind them fast, then either [set them free] with a favor afterwards or [let them go] for a ransom."** (47:4). You have already seen in what preceded that the claim of abrogation is not tenable.
